revision.text: #redirect[[en:Tsuchi (Japanese term)]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

'''犯土'''（つち）は[[選日]]の一つ。
[[庚午]]（[[甲子]]から数えて7番目）から[[丙子]]（13番目）までを'''大犯土'''（おおづち、'''大土'''とも書く）、[[戊寅]]（15番目）から[[甲申]]（21番目）までを'''小犯土'''（こづち、'''小土'''とも書く）という。その間の[[丁丑]]（15番目）を'''犯土間日'''（つちまび）といい、この日は犯土の期間には含まれない。
犯土の期間は土を犯してはならないとされている。市販の[[暦]]では、穴掘り、井戸掘り、種まきなど土いじりは一切慎むべきとされている。

'''犯土'''（つち）は[[選日]]の一つ。
[[庚午]]（[[甲子]]から数えて7番目）から[[丙子]]（13番目）までを'''大犯土'''（おおづち、'''大土'''とも書く）、[[戊寅]]（15番目）から[[甲申]]（21番目）までを'''小犯土'''（こづち、'''小土'''とも書く）という。その間の[[丁丑]]（15番目）を'''犯土間日'''（つちまび）といい、この日は犯土の期間には含まれない。
犯土の期間は土を犯してはならないとされている。市販の[[暦]]では、穴掘り、井戸掘り、種まきなど土いじりは一切慎むべきとされている。
[[category:暦注]]

犯土の期間は土を犯してはならないとされている。市販の[[暦]]では、穴掘り、井戸掘り、種まきなど土いじりは一切慎むべきとされている。
[[category:暦注|つち]]

'''犯土'''（つち）は[[選日]]の一つ。
[[庚午]]（[[甲子]]から数えて7番目）から[[丙子]]（13番目）までを'''大犯土'''（おおづち、'''大土'''とも書く）、[[戊寅]]（15番目）から[[甲申]]（21番目）までを'''小犯土'''（こづち、'''小土'''とも書く）という。その間の[[丁丑]]（14番目）を'''犯土間日'''（つちまび）といい、この日は犯土の期間には含まれない。
犯土の期間は土を犯してはならないとされている。市販の[[暦]]では、穴掘り、井戸掘り、種まきなど土いじりは一切慎むべきとされている。
[[category:暦注|つち]]

'''犯土'''（つち）は[[選日]]の一つ。
[[庚午]]（[[甲子]]から数えて7番目）から[[丙子]]（13番目）までを'''大犯土'''（おおづち、'''大土'''とも書く）、[[戊寅]]（15番目）から[[甲申]]（21番目）までを'''小犯土'''（こづち、'''小土'''とも書く）という。その間の[[丁丑]]（14番目）を'''犯土間日'''（つちまび）といい、この日は犯土の期間には含まれない。
犯土の期間は土を犯してはならないとされている。[[市販]]の[[暦]]では、穴掘り、井戸掘り、種まきなど土いじりは一切慎むべきとされている。
[[category:暦注|つち]]

'''犯土'''（つち）は[[選日]]の一つ。'''土'''、'''椎'''、'''槌'''（発音は同じく「つち」）とも。
[[庚午]]（[[甲子]]から数えて7番目）から[[丙子]]（13番目）までの7日間を'''大犯土'''（おおづち、'''大土'''などとも）、[[戊寅]]（15番目）から[[甲申]]（21番目）までの7日間を'''小犯土'''（こづち、'''小土'''などとも）といい、合わせて犯土という。その間の[[丁丑]]（14番目）を'''犯土間日'''（つちまび）といい、この日は犯土の期間には含まれない。
犯土の期間は土を犯してはならない。つまり、穴掘り、[[井戸]]掘り、[[種まき]]、土木[[工事]]、[[伐採]]など土いじりは一切慎むべきとされている。
{{DEFAULTSORT:つち}} [[category:暦注]]

'''犯土'''（つち、ぼんど）は[[選日]]の一つ。'''土'''、'''椎'''、'''槌'''（読みはいずれも「つち」）とも。
[[庚午]]（[[甲子]]から数えて7番目）から[[丙子]]（13番目）までの7日間を'''大犯土'''（おおづち、'''大土'''などとも）、[[戊寅]]（15番目）から[[甲申]]（21番目）までの7日間を'''小犯土'''（こづち、'''小土'''などとも）という。その間の[[丁丑]]（14番目）を'''間日'''（まび）、'''犯土間日'''（つちまび）、'''中犯土'''（なかづち）といい、この日は犯土には含まれない、あるいは、犯土には含まれても犯土の[[タブー|禁忌]]は存在しない。
犯土の期間には、[[土公神]]（どくじん）が本宮あるいは土中にいるため、土を犯してはならない。つまり、穴掘り、[[井戸]]掘り、[[種まき]]、土木[[工事]]、[[伐採]]など土いじりは一切慎むべきとされている。特に、[[地鎮祭]]等の建築儀礼には凶日とされる。これらの内容は[[土用]]と共通している。
犯土とは元来、これらの「土を犯す行為」、特に、現在の地鎮祭に相当する儀式を意味したが、後に、それを禁じる禁忌、その期間へと意味が変化した。
{{DEFAULTSORT:つち}} [[category:暦注]]

犯土とは元来、これらの「土を犯す行為」、特に、現在の地鎮祭に相当する儀式を意味したが、後に、それを禁じる禁忌、その期間へと意味が変化した。

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.
==References== *{{Cite book|lang=ja |author1=岡田芳朗|authorlink1=岡田芳朗|author2=阿久根末忠|authorlink2=阿久根末忠 |year=1993 |title=現代こよみ読み解き事典 (Modern Almanac Interpretation Dictionary) |publisher=柏書房 (Kashiwa Shobō) |isbn=4-7601-0951-X }}
[[Category:Calendar systems]] [[Category:Astrology]] [[Category:Divination]] [[Category:Mathematics of magic]] [[Category:Onmyōdō]] [[Category:Calendar annotations]]
[[ja:犯土]]

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.
==References== *{{Cite book|lang=ja |author1=岡田芳朗|authorlink1=岡田芳朗|author2=阿久根末忠|authorlink2=阿久根末忠 |year=1993 |title=現代こよみ読み解き事典 (Modern Almanac Interpretation Dictionary) |publisher=柏書房 (Kashiwa Shobō) |isbn=4-7601-0951-X }}

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.

During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.
==References== *{{Cite book|lang=ja |author1=岡田芳朗|authorlink1=岡田芳朗|author2=阿久根末忠|authorlink2=阿久根末忠 |year=1993 |title=現代こよみ読み解き事典 (Modern Almanac Interpretation Dictionary) |publisher=柏書房 (Kashiwa Shobō) |isbn=4-7601-0951-X }}

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.
==References== *{{Cite book|lang=ja |author1=岡田芳朗|authorlink1=岡田芳朗|author2=阿久根末忠|authorlink2=阿久根末忠 |year=1993 |title=現代こよみ読み解き事典 (Modern Almanac Interpretation Dictionary) |publisher=柏書房 (Kashiwa Shobō) |isbn=4-7601-0951-X }}

From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

[[ja:犯土]]

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pages needing attribution]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.
==References== *{{Cite book|lang=ja |author1=岡田芳朗|authorlink1=岡田芳朗|author2=阿久根末忠|authorlink2=阿久根末忠 |year=1993 |title=現代こよみ読み解き事典 (Modern Almanac Interpretation Dictionary) |publisher=柏書房 (Kashiwa Shobō) |isbn=4-7601-0951-X }}

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

term)]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsuchi (Japanese term)}}[[Category:Calendar annotations]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:犯土]]

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

[[ja:犯土]]

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

[[ja:犯土]] [[Category:New]]

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.
==References== *{{Cite book|lang=ja |author1=岡田芳朗|authorlink1=岡田芳朗|author2=阿久根末忠|authorlink2=阿久根末忠 |year=1993 |title=現代こよみ読み解き事典 (Modern Almanac Interpretation Dictionary) |publisher=柏書房 (Kashiwa Shobō) |isbn=4-7601-0951-X }}

From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). {{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日|1=Senjitsu}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.

From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日|1=Senjitsu}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.
==References== *{{Cite book|lang=ja |author1=岡田芳朗|authorlink1=岡田芳朗|author2=阿久根末忠|authorlink2=阿久根末忠 |year=1993 |title=現代こよみ読み解き事典 (Modern Almanac Interpretation Dictionary) |publisher=柏書房 (Kashiwa Shobō) |isbn=4-7601-0951-X }}

From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日|1=Senjitsu}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.
==References== *{{Cite book|lang=ja |author1=岡田芳朗|authorlink1=岡田芳朗|author2=阿久根末忠|authorlink2=阿久根末忠 |year=1993 |title=現代こよみ読み解き事典 (Modern Almanac Interpretation Dictionary) |publisher=柏書房 (Kashiwa Shobō) |isbn=4-7601-0951-X }}

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日|1=Senjitsu}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.
==References== *{{Cite book|lang=ja |author1=岡田芳朗|authorlink1=岡田芳朗|author2=阿久根末忠|authorlink2=阿久根末忠 |year=1993 |title=現代こよみ読み解き事典 (Modern Almanac Interpretation Dictionary) |publisher=柏書房 (Kashiwa Shobō) |isbn=4-7601-0951-X }}

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日|1=Senjitsu}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

[[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsuchi (Japanese term)]][[Category:qq]]

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日|1=Senjitsu}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.
==References== *{{Cite book|lang=ja |author1=岡田芳朗|authorlink1=岡田芳朗|author2=阿久根末忠|authorlink2=阿久根末忠 |year=1993 |title=現代こよみ読み解き事典 (Modern Almanac Interpretation Dictionary) |publisher=柏書房 (Kashiwa Shobō) |isbn=4-7601-0951-X }}

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日|1=Senjitsu}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日|1=Senjitsu}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日|1=Senjitsu}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.
==References== *{{Cite book|lang=ja |author1=岡田芳朗|authorlink1=岡田芳朗|author2=阿久根末忠|authorlink2=阿久根末忠 |year=1993 |title=現代こよみ読み解き事典 (Modern Almanac Interpretation Dictionary) |publisher=柏書房 (Kashiwa Shobō) |isbn=4-7601-0951-X }}

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日|1=Senjitsu}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

{{No footnotes|date=June 2021}} '''Hando''' (犯土) is one of the [[selection days]] ({{ill|selection day|ja|選日|1=Senjitsu}}). Also referred to as '''Tsuchi''', '''Shii''', or '''Tsuchi'''.
From [[Kanoe-no-Uma]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Uma|ja|庚午}}, the seventh day from {{ill|Kinoe-no-Ne|ja|甲子}}) to [[Hinoe-no-Ne]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ne|ja|丙子}}, the thirteenth day), the seven days are called '''Dai-Hando''' (大犯土, "Great Hando" or "Great Earth"). From [[Tsuchinoe-no-Tora]] ({{ill|Tsuchinoe-no-Tora|ja|戊寅}}, the fifteenth day) to [[Kanoe-no-Saru]] ({{ill|Kanoe-no-Saru|ja|甲申}}, the twenty-first day), the seven days are called '''Sho-Hando''' (小犯土, "Small Hando" or "Small Earth"). The intervening day, [[Hinoe-no-Ushi]] ({{ill|Hinoe-no-Ushi|ja|丁丑}}, the fourteenth day), is referred to as '''Kanbi''' (間日, "Intercalary Day"), '''Hando Kanbi''' (犯土間日, "Intercalary Hando Day"), or '''Chu-Hando''' (中犯土, "Middle Hando"). This day is excluded from the Hando period or, alternatively, included but without the taboos associated with Hando.
During the Hando periods, it is believed that the {{ill|Tsuchikami|ja|土公神}} (Earth God) resides in their main shrine or within the earth, and thus the earth must not be disturbed. Activities such as digging, well-digging, sowing seeds, civil engineering works, and tree felling are all prohibited. Particularly for [[Jichinsai]] ({{ill|Jichinsai|ja|地鎮祭}}, ground-breaking ceremonies), Hando is considered an inauspicious time. These beliefs align closely with those associated with {{ill|Doyo|ja|土用}}.
Originally, Hando referred to acts of "disturbing the earth," particularly rituals corresponding to modern-day Jichinsai. Over time, the term evolved to encompass the prohibitions and periods associated with these acts.

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=2021年6月}} '''犯土'''（つち、ぼんど）は[[選日]]の一つ。'''土'''、'''椎'''、'''槌'''。
[[庚午]]（[[甲子]]から数えて7番目）から[[丙子]]（13番目）までの7日間を'''大犯土'''（おおづち、'''大土'''）、[[戊寅]]（15番目）から[[甲申]]（21番目）までの7日間を'''小犯土'''（こづち、'''小土'''）という。その間の[[丁丑]]（14番目）を'''間日'''（まび）、'''犯土間日'''（つちまび）、'''中犯土'''（なかづち）といい、この日は犯土には含まれない。あるいは犯土には含まれても犯土の[[タブー|禁忌]]は存在しない。犯土期間には[[土公神]]（どくじん）が本宮、あるいは土中にいるため土を犯してはならない。穴掘り、井戸掘り、種蒔き、土木工事、伐採など土いじりは全て慎むべきとされている。特に[[地鎮祭]]等の建築儀礼には凶日とされる。これらの内容は[[土用]]と共通する。犯土とは元来、これらの「土を犯す行為」、特に現在の地鎮祭に相当する儀式を意味したが、後にそれを禁じる禁忌やその期間へと意味が変化した。
==参考文献== *{{Cite book|和書 |author1=岡田芳朗|authorlink1=岡田芳朗|author2=阿久根末忠|authorlink2=阿久根末忠 |year = 1993 |title = 現代こよみ読み解き事典 |publisher = 柏書房 |isbn = 4-7601-0951-X }}
{{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:つち}} [[Category:暦法]] [[Category:占星術]] [[Category:占い]] [[Category:術数学]] [[Category:陰陽道]] [[Category:暦注]]

[[庚午]]（[[甲子]]から数えて7番目）から[[丙子]]（13番目）までの7日間を'''大犯土'''（おおづち、'''大土'''）、[[戊寅]]（15番目）から[[甲申]]（21番目）までの7日間を'''小犯土'''（こづち、'''小土'''）という。その間の[[丁丑]]（14番目）を'''間日'''（まび）、'''犯土間日'''（つちまび）、'''中犯土'''（なかづち）といい、この日は犯土には含まれない。あるいは犯土には含まれても犯土の[[タブー|禁忌]]は存在しない。犯土期間には[[土公神]]（どくじん）が本宮、あるいは土中にいるため土を犯してはならない。穴掘り、井戸掘り、種蒔き、土木工事、伐採など土いじりは全て慎むべきとされている。特に[[地鎮祭]]等の建築儀礼には凶日とされる。これらの内容は[[土用]]と共通する。犯土とは元来、これらの「土を犯す行為」、特に現在の地鎮祭に相当する儀式を意味したが、後にそれを禁じる禁忌やその期間へと意味が変化した。
